docs.title: TERRA AI Model Registry Service
docs.text:
This is the documentation site for the TERRA AI Model Registry Service. The service is part of the wider TERRA platform. This is part of the overall platform documentation.

The AI Model Registry service integrates inference models and exposes them via endpoints.

You can use the menu options on the left to navigate through the available documentation. You may be interested to jump directly to an Architecture Overview, see the available Onboarding Material. You can find here the service code repository.

For any questions, you can consult the FAQ, check if there is a relevant issue answering your question or contact the TERRA Help Desk.

docs.title: Service Architecture
docs.text:
The AI Model Registry service provides the foundation for an HTTP-based application that will later integrate inference models for photograph analysis. In its current form, the service acts as a placeholder implementation, establishing the application structure, API conventions, configuration model, logging pipeline, and security integration points required by the final service.

The service is designed to evolve into a component that accepts or references photographic input and executes inference workflows against one or more configured models.

External clients interact with the service through its HTTP API endpoints. The currently available endpoints provide the initial operational surface of the service and are used to validate that the application is correctly deployed, reachable, secured, and ready to be extended with model-driven functionality.

Incoming requests pass through the configured middleware pipeline before reaching the API layer. The logging middleware records request and response information, supporting troubleshooting and operational observability. Security-related request handling is performed through the current-user resolution and token validation components, which identify the calling user and validate the provided authentication token before protected functionality is accessed.

The API layer delegates application operations to the internal service layer. In the current placeholder implementation, the photo service provides the initial abstraction point for photograph-related operations.

The service uses Pydantic schemas to define structured API responses. These schemas provide a clear contract between the HTTP API and consuming clients, ensuring that operational responses and future inference results follow predictable formats. As the service evolves, additional schemas are expected to describe photograph inputs, inference requests, model metadata, inference results, and error responses.

Application setup is centralized in the bootstrap layer. This layer is responsible for creating the FastAPI application instance, configuring logging, registering routes, and wiring the main application dependencies. Runtime configuration is handled through the core configuration module, while dependency construction and access are managed through the container module.

The AI Model Registry service is packaged as a containerized application. Its Dockerfile defines the runtime environment and installs the required Python dependencies listed in the requirements file. The main application entry point initializes the configured application and exposes it for execution by the selected ASGI server.

Operational and troubleshooting logs are generated by the service itself and can be collected by the surrounding platform logging infrastructure. These logs provide visibility into incoming requests, service behavior, errors, and other runtime events. As inference functionality is added, logging can be extended to include model execution events, processing duration, selected model identifiers, request correlation information, and inference failure details.

The current implementation should therefore be considered a scaffold for the final service. Its primary role is to establish the service boundaries and integration patterns early, so that future model-specific functionality can be introduced without changing the external structure of the application.

docs.title: Automations
docs.text: A number of automations are available to facilitate the development, quality assurance, security, deployment, maintenance and onboarding of the service. Here we describe some that are directly, publicly available.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
A GitHub Action workflow is available to generate documentation available in the project in the format presented here. The action is triggered manually and can be executed against the head of the repository. The documentation generated is versioned and the documentation version is expected as input to the workflow.

The documentation is build using the mkdocs library and specifically using the Material for mkdocs plugin. A number of additional tools are ustilized, such as mike to support versioning, and others.

The documentation is generated and tagged with the provided version. It is uploaded to a dedicated documentation branch that is configured to be used as the base branch over which the repository GitHub Page presents its contents.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: The configuration options follow the available configuration files that the ELK (Elastic Search, Logstash, Kibana) stack supports.

docs.title: Secrets
docs.text: Some of the configuration values contain sensitive data and should be treated differently. These may include secrets, connection strings, etc. It is suggested that, depending on the available infrastructure and tooling, the handling of these values is done separately from other configuration values.

docs.title: Data Stores
docs.text: TODO

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text: The service is part of the TERRA platform offered through an existing deployment, following the TERRA release and deployment procedures over a managed infrasrtucture. The purpose of this section is not to detail the deployment processes put in place by the TERRA team.

docs.title: Docker
docs.text: The service is offered as a Docker image through the TERRA GitHub Organization Packages. This process is described in the relevant Automations section.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: In order for the service to operate properly, the needed configuration values must be set to match the environment that it must operate in. The needed configuration is described in the relevant Configuration section.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: For the service to be able to operate, its underpinning services and dependnecies must be available and accessible. The Architecture section describes the ecosystem in which the service needs to operate.

docs.title: Is TERRA really that awsome?
docs.text: Yes, it is.

docs.title: Maintenance
docs.text: The service is part of the TERRA platform offered through an existing deployment, following the TERRA release and deployment procedures over a managed infrasrtucture along with the maintenance activities that are scheduled within the platform. The purpose of this section is not to detail the maintenance activities put in place by the TERRA team.

docs.title: Healthchecks
docs.text:
The service offers an explicit healthcheck endpoint.

An example response that returns 200 OK for healthy state is: 

[
    {
        "status": "healthy"
    }
]

docs.title: Verions & Updates
docs.text: The service follows the versioning and update scheme (Semantic Versions) that the AI Model Registry service supports.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Troubleshooting is primarily done through the logging mechanisms that are available and are described in the respective logging section.

docs.title: Onboarding Material
docs.text: This section contains some references and material that will assist users and integrators onboarding process.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
The definite guide for integrators remains the AI Model Registry Service documentation and API reference that is available here.

The Accounting Service is an open source project and the codebase is available under EUPL-1.2 in the project's GitHub code repository.

On aspect pertaining to security, you can read more at the Security Model section.

For an overall view of the service, you can go through the Architecture.

For any questions, you can consult the FAQ.

If you are facing a problem, check if there is a relevant issue answering your question.

You can always contact us through the TERRA Help Desk.

docs.title: Tutorials
docs.text: You can find useful material and onboarding guidelines in our social channels bellow, as well as our platform documentation.

docs.title: Keep in touch
docs.text:
Make sure to follow the TERRA channels to get the latest news and references:

  • GitHub
  • X
  • YouTube
  • LinkedIn

docs.title: Quality Assurance
docs.text: Key aspects of the Quality Assurance checklist that TERRA services must pass have been defined in the processes and documents governing the platform development and quality assurance.

docs.title: Security
docs.text: Key aspects of the Security checklist and practices that TERRA services must pass have been defined in the processes and documents governing the platform development and quality assurance. In this section we present a selected subset of these that are directly, publicly available and affect the usage and configuration of the service.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
All endpoints exposed by this service require authentication using Bearer tokens in the form of JWTs (JSON Web Tokens). Clients must include a valid token in the Authorization header of each HTTP request, using the following format:

Authorization: Bearer <token>

The service only accepts JWTs that are issued by a trusted identity provider, the TERRA AAI service. This issuer is responsible for authenticating users and issuing tokens with claims that the service can validate. One of the critical claims in the token is the aud (audience) claim. The value of this claim must include the identifier of this service, ensuring that the token was intended to be used with it.

When a token is received, the service performs a series of validation steps before granting access to any endpoint. These steps typically include verifying the token’s signature using the public keys published by the trusted issuer, checking the issuer iss claim to confirm it matches the expected TERRA AAI issuer, validating the audience aud claim to ensure the token was meant for this service, and checking the token expiration exp to confirm the token is still valid. Only if all these checks pass will the request be authenticated and passed for further processing.

The location of the configuration governing the specific behavior is described in the relevant Configuration section.

docs.title: Authorization
docs.text:
When an authenticated call reaches the service, the caller may be authorized to perform an action or not. This will have to be authorized based on the grants that are present as roles in the access token presented as well as the context in which they want to perform the operation.

Within the service, all data access operations as well as individual actions pass authorization checks. The permissions that are checked along with the policies attached to each one is managed in a configuration file that the respective Configuration section describes.

Possible authorization policies include:

  • Context-less assignment: such as an administrator that can perform action X on entity Y, regardless of the kind of affiliation they have with the entity
  • Owner: Specific kind of affiliation between the calling user and the entity over which the action is to be performed, indicating ownership of the entity. An example is an accounting event generator client that is managed by a specific user
  • Client: a policy similar to the Context-less permission assignment. Instead of checking specificaly the "role" claim type, this policy will check the caller client id to have a specific value to match the policy
  • Authenticated: Authenticated user, regardless of any other characteristic vcan be granted or not granted the specific permission
  • Anonymous: Anonymous users be be granted or not granted the specific permission

With respect to Context-less grant assignment, there are two access levels that cen be statically assigned and interpreted for authorization purposes:

  • Admin: Users with admin grant can manage all aspects of the application and see accounting events from all aggregation sources
  • User: Users with user grant can see aggregation events from the aggregation sources they are associated with inside the accounting service

These roles are explicitly managed and assigned within the TERRA AAI service service.

docs.title: Secrets
docs.text: Secrets are a special kind of configuration that requires special handling. This is described in the respective Configuration section.
